nativeRange.habitat: Clear outer lagoons and seaward reefs over hard bottoms, sand, and rubble; typically observed hovering in pairs near a shared burrow at 1–6 m depth

decorPreferences: deep fine live sand bed (7–10 cm)

diet.notes: Naturally feeds by sifting mouthfuls of sand through its gills to extract small benthic crustaceans, copepods, amphipods, mysis, polychaete worms, and even small fish eggs. In captivity, has been reported to accept frozen mysis shrimp, frozen and live brine shrimp, live black worms, and prepared carnivore foods, though some specimens are slow to recognize substitute foods. Generally advised to feed multiple times per day given the species' near-constant sand-sifting and high metabolic demand; a well-established refugium or deep live sand bed with active microfauna populations is commonly considered important for long-term success.

avoidWith: other Valenciennea or similar-bodied sleeper gobies
aggressive or boisterous tankmates that outcompete it for food
small shrimp it may pick at while sand-sifting

summary: An Indo-Pacific sand-sifting goby with a vivid gold head and sapphire-blue cheek stripe. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, tends to form monogamous pairs, and is commonly recommended for mature reef systems with a deep live sand bed and refugium.
careNotes:
Valenciennea strigata is a sand-sifting goby that spends most of its time on or near the substrate, filtering mouthfuls of sand through its gills to extract microfauna. It tends to form lifelong monogamous pairs in the wild and in captivity; a verified bonded pair generally fares better than single specimens, and unrelated conspecifics typically fight on sight.

A mature aquarium with a deep, fine-grained live sand bed (around 7–10 cm) and an attached, well-populated refugium is generally considered important for long-term success. The species is highly vulnerable to starvation in captivity when resident microfauna is depleted, and not all specimens learn to take substitute foods readily — supplemental feeding several times daily with mysis, finely chopped seafood, and frozen meaty fare is commonly advised.

This species is a documented jumper; a tightly-sealed lid or mesh cover is generally advised. Its burrowing behavior can undermine rockwork — securing or epoxying rock structures to the tank bottom (not resting them on sand) is widely recommended to prevent collapse.

Generally considered reef-safe in that it does not consume corals or sessile invertebrates, though corals positioned at or near the substrate may have sand showered onto them as the goby sifts; ReefApp categorises this as 'reef safe with caution' for that reason while LiveAquaria and Reef Builders list it as reef-safe outright.

Cross-source disagreement on minimum tank size: LiveAquaria recommends 30 US gallons (~114 L), Reef Builders recommends ~55 US gallons (~208 L) while emphasising open bottom footprint over volume, and ReefApp recommends 500 L. The chosen value of 200 L (with a 120 cm minimum length) sits between the hobby and specialist recommendations and reflects the species' need for open sand-sifting space rather than column height; reviewers may wish to adjust based on the specific setup. Adult size also varies between sources: LiveAquaria states a maximum of ~15 cm (6 in), while FishBase, ReefApp, and Reef Builders all report 18 cm (7 in) — the 15–18 cm range used here captures both.

FishBase reports a maximum recorded age of only 1 year in the wild, which is widely considered an underestimate for aquarium specimens; aquarium accounts typically suggest 2–5 years with adequate microfauna and water quality.
breedingNotes: Has been documented as a sequential monogamous breeder in the wild: females have been reported to spawn approximately every 13 days, depositing adhesive eggs inside the burrow, with the male guarding the clutch for 2–3 days until hatch. Pair spawning in captivity has been reported occasionally, but rearing the planktonic larvae is generally considered very difficult due to their small size and specialised first-feeding requirements.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care Level Moderate, min 30 gal, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025, max ~6 in, reef-safe yes, peaceful but territorial to conspecifics, sand-sifter, requires populated refugium.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gobiidae/Gobiiformes), max length 18 cm TL, temperature 22–28°C, depth 1–25 m (usually 1–6 m), Indo-Pacific from East Africa to Tuamoto Islands, Ryukyu to Sydney; monogamous, female spawns every ~13 days, male guards eggs 2–3 days; IUCN Least Concern. FishBase's max-age of 1 year is a wild observation and not used here.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Adult 18 cm, recommends 500 L minimum (outlier on the large side), SG 1.026, ~26°C, classified as 'reef safe with caution' due to sand-showering on substrate corals, feed 4+ times daily, best as a male/female pair, jumper, initially shy.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: minimum 55 US gal (~208 L) with emphasis that open bottom footprint matters more than total volume, ~7 in adult, peaceful, monogamous pairs, documented jumper, highly vulnerable to starvation without microfauna replenishment, recommend epoxying rockwork to prevent burrow-induced collapse.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair spawning in captivity has been reported, but rearing the small planktonic larvae is generally considered very challenging.
